content.text: Two Different Concepts That Often Get Confused

An Azure AD tenant and an Azure subscription are related but separate things. Beginners frequently conflate them because both appear in the Azure portal and both contain Azure resources in some sense. Keeping them distinct in your mental model is essential for understanding identity, billing, and access control.

A tenant is an identity authority — a directory that stores users, groups, app registrations, and service principals. A subscription is a billing and resource container — a scope that holds Azure resources and accumulates charges against a payment method. A subscription trusts a tenant for identity, which means any user or service principal in that tenant can potentially be granted access to the subscription's resources via RBAC.

attrs.alt: Azure AD Tenant vs Subscription: containment and trust relationship diagram

content.text: What Is a Tenant?

When your organisation signs up for Microsoft 365, Azure, or any Microsoft cloud service, Azure creates an Azure Active Directory tenant — now called Microsoft Entra ID. The tenant gets a globally unique identifier (the tenant ID, a UUID) and a default domain name in the form <orgname>.onmicrosoft.com. You can add custom domains (contoso.com) on top.
The tenant stores identities: user accounts, service principals, managed identities, guest users.
The tenant defines authentication: sign-in policies, multi-factor authentication, conditional access.
The tenant is the trust root: when a subscription trusts a tenant, it means tokens signed by that tenant's authority are accepted for RBAC evaluation.
One organisation typically has one tenant, though enterprise acquisitions often introduce multiple tenants that later get consolidated.

# View the current tenant ID and default domain
az account show --query "{TenantId:tenantId,Name:name,Subscription:id}" -o table

# List all tenants your account has access to
az account tenant list -o table

# Show tenant details
az rest --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/tenants?api-version=2020-01-01" \
  --query "value[].{TenantId:tenantId,DisplayName:displayName,CountryCode:countryCode}" \
  -o table
Tenant ID vs Directory ID

Tenant ID and Directory ID are the same thing — two names for the same UUID. The Azure portal historically used 'Directory ID' in the Azure AD blade and 'Tenant ID' in subscription settings. Microsoft Entra ID uses 'Tenant ID' exclusively.

# Retrieve tenant ID from multiple sources — they all return the same value
# Method 1: from az account show
az account show --query tenantId -o tsv

# Method 2: from the token claims
az account get-access-token --query "tokenType" -o tsv
python3 -c "
import subprocess, json, base64
token = subprocess.check_output(['az','account','get-access-token','--query','accessToken','-o','tsv']).decode().strip()
payload = token.split('.')[1] + '=='  # add padding
claims = json.loads(base64.b64decode(payload))
print('tid:', claims.get('tid'))
print('iss:', claims.get('iss'))
"

# Method 3: from AAD REST endpoint
az rest --method GET \
  --url "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/organization" \
  --query "value[0].{TenantId:id,DisplayName:displayName,Domain:verifiedDomains[0].name}" \
  -o table
What Is a Subscription?

An Azure subscription is the container that holds your Azure resources and the billing record that accumulates charges. Every resource you create (virtual machine, storage account, database) belongs to exactly one subscription. Subscriptions have offer types (PAYG, EA, MCA, Free Trial) that determine how they are billed.
A subscription has exactly one associated Azure AD tenant — the tenant it trusts for identity.
A subscription has exactly one billing account (EA enrollment, MCA billing profile, or PAYG payment method).
Many subscriptions can trust the same tenant — a common pattern for workload isolation.
A subscription can be moved to a different tenant, but this is a disruptive operation.

# List all subscriptions your account can access
az account list --query "[].{Name:name,Id:id,State:state,TenantId:tenantId}" -o table

# Show full details for the currently active subscription
az account show -o json

# Switch the active subscription context
az account set --subscription "<subscription-name-or-id>"

# Get subscription metadata including management group and billing
az account subscription show --subscription-id "<subscription-id>" -o json
The Trust Relationship: How Tenants and Subscriptions Connect

Every Azure subscription has a property called its associated directory (tenant). This defines which Azure AD tenant the subscription trusts for authentication. When you assign an RBAC role on a subscription, you grant a user or service principal from the trusted tenant the ability to act on the subscription's resources.
One Tenant, Many Subscriptions

A single Azure AD tenant can be trusted by many subscriptions. This is the most common enterprise pattern: one corporate Azure AD tenant (contoso.onmicrosoft.com) used for all user identities, with separate subscriptions for production, development, staging, shared services, and individual teams. Users log in once to the tenant and can then switch between subscriptions they have RBAC access to.

# List all subscriptions trusted by your current tenant
az account list --all --query "[?tenantId=='<your-tenant-id>'].{Name:name,Id:id,State:state}" -o table

# Confirm the tenant a subscription trusts
az account show --subscription "<subscription-id>" --query tenantId -o tsv
One Subscription, One Tenant

A subscription can trust exactly one tenant at a time. You can change which tenant a subscription trusts (subscription transfer to a new directory), but the subscription cannot simultaneously trust two tenants. This is why cross-tenant RBAC is not direct — it requires Azure AD B2B guest users or Lighthouse delegations.

# Attempting to list subscriptions across tenants requires --all flag
# (without --all, az account list only shows subscriptions in the current tenant context)
az account list --all -o table

# Login to a different tenant to see its subscriptions
az login --tenant "<other-tenant-id>"
az account list -o table
Key Differences at a Glance
Dimension
Azure AD Tenant
Azure Subscription
What it stores
Users, groups, service principals, app registrations
Azure resources (VMs, storage, databases, networks)
Primary purpose
Identity and authentication authority
Resource container and billing unit
Billing
No direct billing — identity is free (Azure AD Free)
Accumulates charges; linked to billing account
Cardinality
One per organisation (usually)
Many per organisation, per tenant
Can be deleted?
Yes, but only when empty and no subscriptions trust it
Yes, after removing all resources
Affects RBAC?
Defines who can be granted roles
Defines what they can access (the scope)
Cross-tenant?
Yes, via B2B guest users
No — trusts exactly one tenant
Changing a Subscription's Associated Tenant

Transferring a subscription to a different tenant is sometimes necessary after company acquisitions, team restructuring, or when a developer subscription should move from a personal Microsoft account tenant to a corporate tenant. The operation requires Owner access on the subscription in both the source and destination tenants.
Transferring a subscription's directory removes all existing RBAC role assignments in the subscription. Every role assignment must be recreated in the new tenant after the transfer completes.

# Step 1: Verify current tenant association
az account show --subscription "<sub-id>" --query "{SubscriptionId:id,TenantId:tenantId}" -o table

# Step 2: The directory transfer is initiated in the Azure portal:
# Portal -> Subscriptions -> <subscription> -> Change directory
# (No CLI command currently performs this operation directly)

# Step 3: After the transfer, re-authenticate to the new tenant
az login --tenant "<new-tenant-id>"
az account set --subscription "<sub-id>"

# Step 4: Verify the new tenant association
az account show --query tenantId -o tsv

# Step 5: Recreate RBAC assignments (all were wiped by the transfer)
az role assignment create \
  --role "Owner" \
  --assignee "admin@newtenant.com" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<sub-id>"

az role assignment create \
  --role "Contributor" \
  --assignee "team@newtenant.com" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-prod"
Multiple Tenants: When They Arise
Most organisations have one tenant, but certain scenarios create multiple tenants that must coexist or eventually be consolidated.
Scenario
What Happens
Common Resolution
Company acquisition
Target company has its own Azure AD tenant
Azure AD B2B for cross-tenant access; eventually tenant merge
Developer personal Microsoft account
Personal AAD tenant (e.g. outlook.com) associated with Azure subscription
Transfer subscription to corporate tenant
Microsoft 365 trial
A trial creates a new tenant automatically
Delete trial tenant or move subscriptions to primary tenant
ISV multi-tenant app
App registrations span multiple customer tenants
Use multi-tenant app registrations; no merge needed
Government / sovereign cloud
Commercial and sovereign clouds use separate tenants
Cannot be merged; manage independently

# List tenants your account has access to (useful when you have multiple)
az account tenant list -o table

# Login to a specific tenant context
az login --tenant "<tenant-id>"

# Use --allow-no-subscriptions if a tenant has no subscriptions
# (e.g. an AAD-only tenant for identity management)
az login --tenant "<tenant-id>" --allow-no-subscriptions

# Switch between tenant contexts without re-authenticating
az account set --subscription "<sub-in-other-tenant>"
# (will automatically switch tenant context if the subscription is in a different tenant)
Azure Lighthouse: Cross-Tenant Resource Management

Azure Lighthouse lets a service provider (MSP or internal platform team) manage resources in a customer tenant's subscriptions without requiring a guest user account in that tenant. Delegated Resource Management projects a subscription's scope into the managing tenant using a Lighthouse delegation, which persists across subscription moves and billing changes.

# Deploy a Lighthouse delegation (run in the customer subscription)
az deployment sub create \
  --location eastus \
  --template-uri https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Azure/Azure-Lighthouse-samples/master/templates/delegated-resource-management/delegatedResourceManagement.json \
  --parameters '{
    "mspOfferName":        {"value": "Contoso MSP"},
    "mspOfferDescription": {"value": "Managed services delegation"},
    "managedByTenantId":   {"value": "<msp-tenant-id>"},
    "authorizations": {"value": [
      {
        "principalId": "<msp-group-object-id>",
        "roleDefinitionId": "b24988ac-6180-42a0-ab88-20f7382dd24c",
        "principalIdDisplayName": "MSP Contributors"
      }
    ]}
  }'

# From the MSP tenant, list delegated subscriptions
az managedservices assignment list -o table

# Access a customer subscription from the MSP tenant
az account set --subscription "<customer-subscription-id>"
az resource list --subscription "<customer-subscription-id>" -o table
Tenant-Level vs Subscription-Level Operations
Many Azure operations are scoped to the tenant layer, not the subscription layer. Knowing which layer an operation belongs to prevents confusion about where to look and what permissions are required.
Operation
Scope
Required Role
Create a user account
Tenant (Azure AD)
User Administrator or Global Administrator
Register an application
Tenant (Azure AD)
Application Administrator or Cloud App Admin
Create a service principal
Tenant (Azure AD)
Application Administrator
Set conditional access policy
Tenant (Azure AD)
Conditional Access Administrator
Create a resource group
Subscription
Contributor or Owner on the subscription
Assign a subscription RBAC role
Subscription
Owner or User Access Administrator
Create a budget
Subscription
Cost Management Contributor
Set a management group policy
Management Group (above subscription)
Policy Contributor at MG scope

# Tenant-level operations: use az ad commands (Microsoft Graph)
# Create a user (tenant scope)
az ad user create \
  --display-name "Alice Smith" \
  --user-principal-name alice@contoso.onmicrosoft.com \
  --password "TempPass1!" \
  --force-change-password-next-sign-in true

# Register an app (tenant scope)
az ad app create --display-name "myapp-prod" --sign-in-audience AzureADMyOrg

# Subscription-level operations: use az commands with --subscription
# Create a resource group (subscription scope)
az group create --name "rg-prod" --location eastus --subscription "<sub-id>"

# Assign a role (subscription scope)
az role assignment create \
  --role "Contributor" \
  --assignee alice@contoso.onmicrosoft.com \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<sub-id>"
Managed Identities: Where Tenant and Subscription Meet

Managed identities are a key example of how tenants and subscriptions interlock. A managed identity is created in the subscription (as part of a resource like a VM or Function App), but its service principal lives in the tenant. This means the managed identity's principal ID is a tenant-level object, and permissions granted to it (Key Vault access, Storage role assignments) are evaluated at the tenant/RBAC layer.
System-Assigned Managed Identity

# Enable system-assigned identity on a VM (subscription-level operation)
az vm identity assign \
  --name myvm \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --subscription "<sub-id>"

# Get the principal ID — this is a tenant-level service principal
PRINCIPAL_ID=$(az vm show \
  --name myvm \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --query "identity.principalId" -o tsv)

echo "Service Principal (tenant object): $PRINCIPAL_ID"

# Look it up in Azure AD (tenant layer)
az ad sp show --id "$PRINCIPAL_ID" --query "{DisplayName:displayName,AppId:appId,Type:servicePrincipalType}" -o table

# Grant it subscription-level RBAC (subscription layer)
az role assignment create \
  --role "Storage Blob Data Reader" \
  --assignee "$PRINCIPAL_ID" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-prod/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/mystorage"
User-Assigned Managed Identity

# Create a user-assigned managed identity (subscription resource, tenant principal)
az identity create \
  --name "myapp-identity" \
  --resource-group "rg-identities" \
  --subscription "<sub-id>"

# The identity has both a resource ID (subscription layer) and a principal ID (tenant layer)
az identity show \
  --name "myapp-identity" \
  --resource-group "rg-identities" \
  --query "{ResourceId:id,PrincipalId:principalId,ClientId:clientId,TenantId:tenantId}" \
  -o table

# Assign the identity to a VM
az vm identity assign \
  --name myvm \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --identities /subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-identities/providers/Microsoft.ManagedIdentity/userAssignedIdentities/myapp-identity

# Grant Key Vault access using the identity's tenant principal ID
PRINCIPAL_ID=$(az identity show --name myapp-identity --resource-group rg-identities --query principalId -o tsv)
az role assignment create \
  --role "Key Vault Secrets User" \
  --assignee "$PRINCIPAL_ID" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-prod/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/myvault"
Tenant and Subscription in the ARM URL Hierarchy
Azure Resource Manager exposes every resource through a predictable URL path. Understanding the path structure clarifies the relationship between tenants, subscriptions, and resources.

# ARM URL hierarchy (REST API paths)

# Tenant root (all management operations begin here)
# GET https://management.azure.com/tenants?api-version=2020-01-01

# Subscription list under a tenant (your account's accessible subscriptions)
# GET https://management.azure.com/subscriptions?api-version=2020-01-01

# A specific subscription
# GET https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<sub-id>?api-version=2020-01-01

# Resource group inside a subscription
# GET https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/<rg>?api-version=2021-04-01

# A resource inside the resource group
# GET https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/<rg>/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/<vm>?api-version=2023-03-01

# Notice: the tenant ID is NOT in the ARM path — it is encoded in the bearer token instead.
# The token's 'tid' claim tells ARM which tenant the caller is authenticated from.
TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --query accessToken -o tsv)
python3 -c "
import sys, json, base64
tok = '$TOKEN'
payload = tok.split('.')[1]
payload += '=' * (4 - len(payload) % 4)
claims = json.loads(base64.b64decode(payload))
print('Caller tenant (tid):', claims['tid'])
print('Subscription access granted for:', '$(az account show --query id -o tsv)')
"
Graph API: Tenant-Scoped Operations

# Microsoft Graph API is tenant-scoped (not subscription-scoped)
# Base URL: https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/

# List all users in the tenant
az rest --method GET \
  --url "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users" \
  --headers "ConsistencyLevel=eventual" \
  --query "value[0:5].{UPN:userPrincipalName,DisplayName:displayName}" \
  -o table

# List all groups in the tenant
az rest --method GET \
  --url "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/groups" \
  --query "value[].{Id:id,Name:displayName,Type:groupTypes[0]}" \
  -o table

# List service principals (includes managed identities and app registrations)
az rest --method GET \
  --url "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/servicePrincipals" \
  --query "value[?servicePrincipalType=='ManagedIdentity'].{Id:id,DisplayName:displayName}" \
  -o table
Common Misconceptions
Misconception
Reality
Deleting a subscription deletes the tenant
Subscriptions and tenants are independent. You can delete a subscription and the tenant persists with its users and app registrations.
Creating a new subscription creates a new tenant
New subscriptions in the Azure portal default to your existing tenant. A new tenant is only created if you explicitly choose to create one.
Every subscription has its own user directory
All subscriptions in an organisation typically share one tenant. User accounts exist in the tenant, not per-subscription.
Subscription Owners can manage Azure AD users
Subscription Owner is an Azure RBAC role (resource scope). Managing users requires Azure AD roles like User Administrator, which are tenant-scoped and separate from Azure RBAC.
Service principals are per-subscription
Service principals (including managed identities) are tenant objects. They can be granted roles in many subscriptions simultaneously.
Working With Multiple Tenants in the CLI and SDKs

If you manage resources across multiple Azure AD tenants (common in MSP scenarios, post-acquisition environments, or when you have both personal and corporate Azure accounts), you need to understand how the Azure CLI and SDKs handle tenant context switching.
Multi-Tenant CLI Login

# Login to the first tenant (corporate)
az login --tenant "contoso.onmicrosoft.com"
az account list -o table  # shows subscriptions in the contoso tenant

# Login to a second tenant without replacing the first session
az login --tenant "fabrikam.onmicrosoft.com"
az account list -o table  # now shows both tenants' subscriptions (if you have access)

# Switch the active context to a subscription in the second tenant
az account set --subscription "<fabrikam-subscription-id>"
az account show --query "{Sub:name,Tenant:tenantId}" -o table

# Explicitly target a tenant for a single command (without switching context)
az resource list \
  --subscription "<contoso-sub-id>" \
  --tenant "contoso.onmicrosoft.com" \
  -o table
Multi-Tenant Authentication with the Python SDK

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential, AzureCliCredential
from azure.mgmt.resource import ResourceManagementClient, SubscriptionClient

# DefaultAzureCredential picks up the active az CLI context (tenant + subscription)
credential = DefaultAzureCredential()

# Get all subscriptions accessible to the authenticated identity (across tenants)
sub_client = SubscriptionClient(credential)
for sub in sub_client.subscriptions.list():
    print(f"Subscription: {sub.display_name:40} Tenant: {sub.tenant_id}  State: {sub.state}")

# Operate on resources in a specific subscription
resource_client = ResourceManagementClient(credential, subscription_id="<sub-id>")
for rg in resource_client.resource_groups.list():
    print(f"  RG: {rg.name:30} Location: {rg.location}")

# When managing cross-tenant resources, use a tenant-specific credential
from azure.identity import ClientSecretCredential

# Tenant A credential (service principal in tenant A)
tenant_a_cred = ClientSecretCredential(
    tenant_id="<tenant-a-id>",
    client_id="<app-id-in-tenant-a>",
    client_secret="<secret>"
)

# Tenant B credential (service principal in tenant B)
tenant_b_cred = ClientSecretCredential(
    tenant_id="<tenant-b-id>",
    client_id="<app-id-in-tenant-b>",
    client_secret="<secret>"
)

# Resource clients pointing to different subscriptions in different tenants
from azure.mgmt.resource import ResourceManagementClient

client_a = ResourceManagementClient(tenant_a_cred, subscription_id="<sub-in-tenant-a>")
client_b = ResourceManagementClient(tenant_b_cred, subscription_id="<sub-in-tenant-b>")

# List VMs from both tenants in parallel
import concurrent.futures
def list_vms(client, label):
    vms = list(client.resources.list(filter="resourceType eq 'Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines'"))
    return label, vms

with concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor() as ex:
    results = list(ex.map(lambda x: list_vms(*x), [(client_a, "Tenant A"), (client_b, "Tenant B")]))
    for label, vms in results:
        print(f"{label}: {len(vms)} VMs")
Tenant and Subscription in Azure Policy

Azure Policy can be assigned at the management group, subscription, or resource group scope. All policy assignments and definitions that reference identity (RBAC conditions, DeployIfNotExists remediation identities) must resolve against the same tenant that owns the subscription.

# Assign a built-in policy at subscription scope
az policy assignment create \
  --name "require-tags" \
  --display-name "Require Environment tag on all resources" \
  --policy /providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/96670d01-0a4d-4649-9c89-2d3abc0a5025 \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<sub-id>" \
  --params '{"tagName": {"value": "Environment"}}'

# List all policy assignments in the subscription
az policy assignment list \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<sub-id>" \
  --query "[].{Name:name,Policy:policyDefinitionId,Scope:scope}" \
  -o table

# Check compliance state for a subscription
az policy state list \
  --subscription "<sub-id>" \
  --filter "complianceState eq 'NonCompliant'" \
  --query "[].{Resource:resourceId,Policy:policyDefinitionName,State:complianceState}" \
  -o table | head -20

# Trigger a compliance re-evaluation (on-demand scan)
az policy state trigger-scan --subscription "<sub-id>"
echo "Scan triggered. Results available in ~5 minutes."
az policy state summarize --subscription "<sub-id>" -o table
Summary

A tenant is an identity directory that stores users, groups, and application identities. A subscription is a billing and resource container. A subscription trusts exactly one tenant — meaning identities from that tenant can be granted RBAC access to the subscription's resources. One tenant can be trusted by many subscriptions, which is the standard enterprise pattern for workload isolation without identity silos. Transferring a subscription to a different tenant resets all RBAC assignments. Azure Lighthouse enables cross-tenant management for MSP and platform team scenarios without requiring guest user accounts.
